question_title: Will the interest in “uscis pause” change between 2026-05-04 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?
summary: The search term "uscis pause" hit its peak (100) on May 2, 2026, driven by the convergence of a new FBI enhanced vetting pause (effective April 27) that halted all fingerprint-based immigration adjudications, two federal court rulings blocking the USCIS pause (May 1), and extensive media coverage. The question compares Google Trends values on May 4 (2 days after peak, still elevated) vs May 16 (14 days after peak, substantially decayed) within the Apr 16 - May 16 window. Academic research shows 70-80% of attention is lost within 24 hours and 90-97% within one week for event-driven spikes. Even accounting for the ongoing nature of this policy crisis (affecting millions, active litigation, no end date on FBI vetting pause), Monte Carlo simulations and scenario-weighted analysis consistently show "Decreases" as the dominant outcome. The main risk is a major new development near May 16 that generates a spike exceeding the May 2 peak, but this requires specific timing and magnitude that is unlikely.

factors.description: Peak at 100 on May 2, comparing May 4 (2 days after peak) vs May 16 (14 days after peak) - natural decay strongly favors decrease
Academic research shows 70-80% attention lost within 24 hours, 90-97% within one week for event-driven spikes (Lorenz-Spreen et al. 2019, Wu & Huberman 2007)
This is an ongoing policy issue affecting millions (FBI vetting pause, travel ban litigation), not a one-time event - sustained interest reduces decay rate
Active litigation with multiple courts ruling (Kobick May 1, Russell April) - new developments could generate fresh search interest near May 16
New FBI vetting pause (April 27) is brand new and ongoing with no end date - people continue discovering they're affected
Monte Carlo simulation with stretched exponential decay + 25% re-spike probability shows 91-95% probability of decrease across scenarios
Even if a new spike occurs, it must happen ON or near May 16 specifically to flip the outcome - narrow timing window reduces probability of Increases
Google Trends rescaling: if a bigger spike occurs before May 16, it rescales down May 4 value, potentially reducing the gap or flipping to Increases

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary:**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| **Decreases** | **82%** |
| Increases | 10% |
| Doesn't change | 8% |

**Key reasoning:** "uscis pause" peaked at 100 on May 2, 2026, driven by a convergence of events: a new FBI enhanced vetting pause (April 27) halting all fingerprint-based immigration adjudications, two federal court rulings blocking USCIS pauses (May 1), and extensive media coverage. The comparison is May 4 (2 days after peak, still elevated) vs May 16 (14 days after peak). Even accounting for the ongoing nature of this policy crisis and active litigation, the 12-day gap strongly favors decay. The main risk is a major new spike near May 16 exceeding the original peak, but this requires both specific timing and magnitude that is improbable.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “uscis pause” change between 2026-05-04 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43387)

[BREAKING: USCIS Holds Temporary Pause on Green Card Applications for Enhanced Security Checks](https://www.visalawyerblog.com/breaking-uscis-holds-temporary-pause-on-green-card-applications-for-enhanced-security-checks/)
[Judge rejects Trump administration halt on immigration applications](https://thedailyrecord.com/2026/05/01/judge-blocks-trump-immigration-application-halt/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=uscis%20pause&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=uscis%20pause&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16) for 2026-05-16 is within 3 of the value at 2026-05-04. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "uscis pause",
  "date": "2026-04-16 2026-05-16",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.

input.assessment: Revised assessment: The scenario-weighted analysis gives ~87% for Decreases, which I've moderated slightly to 82% to account for the unusual nature of this particular USCIS situation (ongoing crisis affecting millions, active multi-court litigation, brand new FBI vetting policy with no end date). The core driver is simple: the peak was May 2, May 4 is 2 days after peak (still elevated), May 16 is 14 days after peak (substantially decayed). Even generous re-spike assumptions don't materially change the conclusion because the re-spike would need to happen specifically near May 16 to flip the outcome.
input.tool_audit: Web search: Very useful for identifying the specific events (FBI vetting pause, court rulings). Research sub-agent: Provided excellent academic background on decay patterns. Code sandbox: Essential for Monte Carlo and scenario analysis. Google Trends direct data: Not accessible (JS-rendered page).
input.process_reflection: The analysis was straightforward once I identified the key dynamics. The main gap was inability to access actual Google Trends values for May 4, which would have allowed a much more precise estimate.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All factor claims are well-grounded in the research trace:

- **Factor 1** (Peak at 100 on May 2): Confirmed by question background: "The current value of the topic 'uscis pause' at the time of writing this question (May 2, 2026) compared to the last 30 days is 100."
- **Factor 2** (70-80% attention lost in 24hrs, 90-97% within one week): Confirmed by the comprehensive Google Trends decay research sub-agent output, citing Lorenz-Spreen et al. (2019), Wu & Huberman (2007), and Candia et al. (2019). The trace contains detailed summaries of these papers with specific figures.
- **Factor 3** (Ongoing policy issue affecting millions): Confirmed by NPR ("millions of professionals risk losing legal status"), Newsweek (250+ immigrants in court cases), and multiple law firm articles in the trace's web search results.
- **Factor 4** (Active litigation, multiple courts ruling): Confirmed by trace — Judge Kobick (Boston, May 1) and Judge Russell (Maryland) rulings both documented via web searches and fetch_url results.
- **Factor 5** (FBI vetting pause April 27, no end date): Confirmed by Fragomen (Apr 29), Morgan Lewis (Apr 30), VisaLawyerBlog (Apr 30), and CBS News articles in the trace. The fetch_url on VisaLawyerBlog explicitly states "Expected Duration: Unknown."
- **Factor 6** (Monte Carlo 91-95%): Simulation outputs in trace show 94.5% Decreases at 25% respike and 91.3% at 40% respike — "91-95%" is accurate.
- **Factor 7** (Narrow timing window): Analytical judgment, correctly reasoned.
- **Factor 8** (Google Trends rescaling): Correctly identified and analyzed in trace code execution.

### Double-Counting — MINOR (not rising to warn)
Factor 6 (Monte Carlo simulation) encodes the same decay assumptions as Factors 1 and 2 (the simulation uses stretched exponential models with half-life parameters from the same academic literature). This slightly inflates the apparent evidence base but doesn't change the directional forecast, and the agent's final probability (82%) is well below the simulation output (94.5%), suggesting the agent isn't mechanically aggregating logits.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs match descriptions. Decreases factors have positive logits pushing toward that outcome. The Doesn't change and Increases factors correctly push toward their respective named outcomes.

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
Assessment concludes 82% Decreases, consistent with factors overwhelmingly favoring Decreases. The 5pp discount from the scenario-weighted 87% is justified by the agent's qualitative reasoning about the unusual policy context.

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent properly identifies the ±3 threshold, May 4 vs May 16 comparison dates, the April 16-May 16 resolution window, SerpAPI as authoritative source, daily precision, and UTC dates. The Monte Carlo explicitly models these resolution rules.

### Probability Assessment — PASS
My independent estimate: ~85-88% Decreases, ~6-8% Doesn't change, ~6-8% Increases. The agent's 82/8/10 is slightly more conservative than mine but within a reasonable range (~4-6pp gap, not enough for a fail). The core logic is sound: May 4 is 2 days after peak (still elevated), May 16 is 14 days after peak (substantially decayed). Even accounting for the ongoing policy nature, active litigation, and new FBI vetting pause, the structural argument for decline is strong. The agent correctly identifies that a re-spike would need to happen specifically near May 16 to flip the outcome.

The comparison with similar concurrent forecasts provides useful context: the agent gave 92% Decreases for "british royal family" (one-time state visit event) and 95% for "anna wintour" (Met Gala peak). The lower 82% for USCIS is directionally appropriate given the ongoing policy nature, but I think the discount is slightly too large — "uscis pause" is still primarily a news-cycle-driven query, and the FBI vetting pause has no scheduled end-date announcement that would generate a specific May 16 spike. Still, the gap isn't large enough to fail.

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
The worldview research entry for USCIS (f3527fea) explicitly states all web research tools were blocked and findings are based on pre-2025 knowledge. The main agent supplemented this successfully with its own web searches that found current information about the FBI vetting pause and court rulings. No contradictions. No worldview forecast entries exist for USCIS to check numeric consistency against.

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is genuinely strong. It constructs 5 specific mechanisms: (1) multi-layered ongoing crisis vs. one-time event, (2) escalating legal environment with specific examples, (3) slow discovery of FBI pause affecting hundreds of thousands, (4) May 4 Sunday baseline effect, (5) Google Trends rescaling risk. It names specific conditions that would shift the estimate (actual May 4 value, scheduled court rulings, sustained search behavior, protests/hearings). This is a real alternative story.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence of 0.55 is appropriately modest given: inability to access actual Google Trends data (JS rendering failed), reliance on decay models that may not perfectly apply to sustained policy issues, genuine uncertainty about the May 4 value, and forward-looking uncertainty about new legal developments.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent claims "Google Trends direct data: Not accessible (JS-rendered page)" — confirmed by trace (fetch_url returned browser compatibility warning, no data). Ground truth shows 6 fetch_url calls with 1 error (16.7%) and 7 web_search calls at 0% error rate — consistent with agent's narrative. The 1 error among 35 total calls (2.9%) is minor and acknowledged.

### Pre-Publication Event — N/A
Forward-looking question comparing two future dates from published_at (May 4).

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
The Monte Carlo (93-95%) and scenario-weighted analysis (87%) serve as implicit anchors. The final 82% is below both but the departure is explained by the agent's qualitative assessment of USCIS-specific factors (ongoing crisis, active litigation, no end date). The 5pp gap from the scenario-weighted anchor is minimal.

## Summary
The forecast is well-constructed with thorough evidence gathering, appropriate Monte Carlo modeling, and good engagement with the specific USCIS policy context. All factual claims are grounded in the trace. The probability (82% Decreases) is slightly more conservative than my estimate (~86%) but within acceptable range. The counterargument is strong and self-confidence is appropriately calibrated. No errors found requiring action.
